title: Perfect transmission and parallel composition for quantum walks on graphs with two leads
authors: Allan John Gerrard, Ryo Asaka, Kazumitsu Sakai
abstract: We study scattering for continuous-time quantum walks on finite graphs with two attached leads. We derive explicit formulae for the two-terminal scattering matrix in terms of characteristic polynomials of the finite graph and its vertex-deleted subgraphs. For real-weighted two-terminal graphs, we then introduce three real quantities, $μ_1$, $μ_2$, and $ν$, which are each additive under parallel composition of graphs. In these variables, perfect transmission at fixed momentum is characterized by the condition $μ_1=μ_2$ together with a hyperbola in the corresponding $(μ,ν)$-plane, whose points determine the transmission phase. This turns the search for graphs with prescribed transmission properties into a geometric vector-sum problem for smaller building blocks.
summary: The paper develops a method to design quantum graphs with specific scattering properties by decomposing them into simpler components. By mapping transmission conditions to a geometric vector-sum problem, the authors show how to construct complex networks that act as perfect momentum filters or phase gates.
main_problem: The challenge of constructing finite graphs with prescribed scattering properties, such as perfect transmission or specific phase gates, for continuous-time quantum walks.
main_result: The authors introduce three additive quantities (mu1, mu2, and nu) that transform the search for specific transmission properties into a geometric vector-sum problem in a (mu, nu)-plane.
method: The study uses the S-matrix formalism, Schur complements, and characteristic polynomials of graphs and their subgraphs, alongside a parallel composition rule for graph building.
model_or_system: Continuous-time quantum walks on finite, edge-weighted graphs connected to two semi-infinite paths (leads), where the Hamiltonian is the adjacency matrix of the infinite graph.
key_observables: S-matrix elements (reflection and transmission amplitudes), transmission probability, transmission phase, and effective length.
important_parameters: Momentum (k), edge weights (omega), and the admittance-like parameters (mu1, mu2, nu).
main_assumptions: The graph G is assumed to be connected; the edge weights are real-weighted for the geometric construction.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the process of extending a graph up a lead; Figure 2 illustrates graph extension; Figure 3 visualizes the vector sum of paths landing on a hyperbola; Figure 4 shows a specific vector sum achieving perfect transmission; Figure 5 illustrates a phase gate setup.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the scattering problem and S-matrix in terms of graph polynomials, introduces the additive mu-nu parameters for parallel composition, provides a geometric framework for finding perfect transmission, and concludes with practical examples of constructing phase gates and calculating effective lengths.
why_interesting: This work provides a systematic, modular approach to designing quantum networks and gates by treating complex graph structures as combinations of simple, additive building blocks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Visual description of the operation of extending the graph G up the first lead.
Figure 2: The hyperbola (24) in the µ-ν plane. The points of the hyperbola are parametrised by the phase of transmission θ ∈(−π, 0) ∪(0, π).

Figure 3: A visual explanation of the perfect transmission property of a two-terminal graph consisting of parallel paths at momentum k = −π/4. Each path is represented as a vector in the µ-ν plane, and their parallel composition corresponds to the sum of these vectors, landing precisely on the perfect transmission hyperbola.

Figure 4: Left: the graph obtained by combining graphs G(1) and G(2) in parallel. Right: a graphical explanation of perfect transmission exhibited by G at k = −π/6. The vector (µ, ν) for each graph is plotted as a blue line. The sum of the two vectors for G(1) and G(2) lands on the perfect transmission hyperbola, indicated by the blue star.
Figure 5: Phase gate for operating momentum k = −π/4. The resulting phase angle is equal to ψ = arccos(−1/3) −π/2 ≈0.108173π.
